revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:碧血碑]]
{{Referencesの明記|date=2019-04-19}} {{特殊文字|説明=[[CJK Unified Ideographs Extension A]]}} {{Infobox artifact | name = Hekketsuhi (碧血碑) | image = 碧血碑.jpg | image_caption = | material = Stone | area = | writing = | created = 1875 | discovered = | location = 1 Yachigashira-chō, Hakodate-shi, Hokkaido{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=250}} | id = }}
'''Hekketsuhi''' (碧血碑) is a {{ill|Memorial|en|Memorial|qid=Q5003624|lt=Memorial|1=Memorial}} erected in {{ill|Hakodate|en|Hakodate|qid=Q26418|lt=Hakodate|1=Hakodate}}, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], Japan, at the base of {{ill|Mount Hakodate|en|Mount Hakodate|qid=Q3526214|lt=Mount Hakodate|1=Mount Hakodate}}. Built in May 1875 during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, it honors the fallen soldiers of the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|qid=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}}, particularly those who perished in the {{ill|Battle of Hakodate|en|Battle of Hakodate|qid=Q716562|lt=Battle of Hakodate|1=Battle of Hakodate}}. It commemorates approximately 800 soldiers from the [[former Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate forces]], including notable figures like [[Toshizō Hijikata]] and [[Saburōsuke Nakajima]].
The term "碧血" (Hekketsu, "jade blood") originates from the "Outer Chapters" of the {{ill|Zhuangzi (book)|en|Zhuangzi (book)|qid=Q1074987|lt=Zhuangzi|1=Zhuangzi (book)}}: "Chang Hong died in Shu. His blood, after three years, turned into jade." This legend suggests that the blood of the loyal and virtuous transforms into jade after three years underground.
== Structure == The memorial is approximately 6 meters tall. The stone was sourced from {{ill|Izu|en|Izu|qid=Q226457|lt=Izu|1=Izu}}, crafted in Tokyo's [[Reiganjima]], and transported by sea. While the inscriptions are attributed to [[Keisuke Ōtori]], this remains uncertain.
On the reverse of the monument, the inscription reads: "明治辰巳實有此事 立石山上{{拡張漢字|A|㕥}}表厥志," which translates to, "In the year Meiji 2, this event indeed occurred. A stone was erected on the mountain to express this sentiment." Meiji 2 (1869) marks the surrender of the Tokugawa forces at {{ill|Goryōkaku|en|Goryōkaku|qid=Q1196357|lt=Goryōkaku|1=Goryōkaku}} and the conclusion of the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|qid=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}}. The ambiguous phrasing reflects the sensitivity of acknowledging these events during the period.
== History == [[File:柳川熊吉翁之寿碑.jpg|thumb|200px|right|Yanagawa Kumakichi Memorial]] During the {{ill|Battle of Hakodate|en|Battle of Hakodate|qid=Q716562|lt=Battle of Hakodate|1=Battle of Hakodate}}, fallen Tokugawa soldiers were labeled as "rebels," and their remains were left unburied to decompose. [[Kumakichi Yanagawa]], a local figure, resolved to collect and bury the bodies. Collaborating with the chief priest of [[Jikkō-ji]] Temple, Nichiryū Matsuo, and carpenter master [[Sukemono Ōoka]], he arranged for his followers to gather the remains and bury them temporarily at Jikkō-ji, Shōmyō-ji, and Jōgen-ji.
Yanagawa, originally from {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}}, had moved to Hakodate in {{ill|1856|en|1856|qid=Q7676|lt=1856|1=1856}} and worked as a contractor supplying laborers, notably during the construction of {{ill|Goryōkaku|en|Goryōkaku|qid=Q1196357|lt=Goryōkaku|1=Goryōkaku}}. He maintained close ties with Tokugawa officials like [[:en:Takeaki Enomoto|Takeaki Enomoto]].
Yanagawa's burial efforts violated government orders prohibiting the memorialization of "rebel" forces, leading to his arrest. However, his dignified stance swayed officials, resulting in a tacit approval of the burials. [[Keizō Tajima]], a {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|qid=Q676187|lt=Satsuma Domain|1=Satsuma Domain}} officer, even advocated against executing Yanagawa, asserting his importance for Japan's future.
In 1871, Yanagawa purchased land on Mount Hakodate, transferring the remains from Jikkō-ji to this site. In 1874, the Meiji government formally lifted restrictions on commemorating fallen Tokugawa soldiers. With support from figures like Enomoto and Ōtori, Yanagawa erected the Hekketsuhi in May 1875.
Yanagawa spent his final years near the monument, passing away in {{ill|1913|en|1913|qid=Q2080|lt=1913|1=1913}} at the age of 89. In his honor, a separate memorial was built nearby in 1912 to commemorate his deeds.
Today, memorial services are held at the monument during events like the Hakodate Goryōkaku Festival.<ref name="はこぶら碧血碑"/>
== Access == * From [[:en:Hakodate City Tram|Hakodate City Tram]] "Yachigashira" stop: 15-minute walk.<ref name="はこぶら碧血碑"/> * From [[Hakodate Bus]] "Yachigashira" stop.<ref>[https://www.city.hakodate.hokkaido.jp/docs/2014012201148/ "Stop Name: Y26 Yachigashira"] - Hakodate City website, accessed May 19, 2019.</ref>
== Another "Hekketsuhi" == A second memorial bearing the same name was erected in {{ill|Assabu, Hokkaido|en|Assabu, Hokkaido|qid=Q1354252|lt=Assabu|1=Assabu, Hokkaido}} in {{ill|1919|en|1919|qid=Q2157|lt=1919|1=1919}} at the site of the [[Inakuraishi Battlefield]], where Tokugawa and [[Matsumae Domain]] forces clashed on November 12, 1868. This memorial, established by former Matsumae retainers, was later relocated due to the construction of the {{ill|Uzura Dam|en|Uzura Dam|qid=Q34969688|lt=Uzura Dam|1=Uzura Dam}}.<ref>[http://www.hiyama.pref.hokkaido.lg.jp/ss/srk/tabi/08assabu/parts/3806ink.htm "Inakuraishi Battlefield and Hekketsuhi"] - Hiyama Subprefectural Office, accessed April 19, 2014.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Goryōkaku|en|Goryōkaku|qid=Q1196357|lt=Goryōkaku|1=Goryōkaku}} * [[Hakodate Gokoku Shrine]]
== External Links == * [http://www.hakobura.jp/db/db-view/2010/05/post-133.html Official Hakodate Tourism Information "Hako-bura" - Hekketsuhi]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{coord|41|45|5.6|N|140|42|31.6|E|region:JP|display=title}}
